coredata.dc:title: Electrochemical detection of isocarbophos in apple and cabbage with Ti3C2TX/arginine and serine-functionalized graphene quantum dot@gold nanostar aerogel and target cycle dual signal amplification
coredata.dc:creator: Ruiyi, Li
Nan, Wang
Yirui, Shen
Guangli, Wang
Zaijun, Li
coredata.dc:description: The paper reports one strategy for synthesis of Ti3C2TX/arginine and serine-functionalized graphene quantum dot@Au nanostar aerogel (Ti3C2TX/RS-GQD@Au). Positively charged RS-GQDs bound with negatively charged Ti3C2TX sheets to form Ti3C2TX aerogel. Then, HAuCl4 was in situ reduced with ascorbic acid in the presence of L-glutathione to form gold nanostars on Ti3C2TX sheets. The resulting Ti3C2TX/RS-GQD@Au shows one three-dimensional structure. The gold nanostars were homogeneously dispersed on Ti3C2TX sheets. Introduction of RS-GQD realizes construction of Schottky heterojunction. The presence of L-glutathione triggers an asymmetric structural evolution of gold nanocrystals and leads to more exposed high-index crystal facets. Unique structure achieves to high conductivity, structural stability and catalytic activity. Ti3C2TX/RS-GQD@Au was used for construction of electrochemical sensor for detection of isocarbophos coupled with target cycle amplification. The peak current at 0.27 V linearly increases with the increase of isocarbophos concentration between 1 × 10−11 and 8 × 10−16 M with 2.9 × 10−16 M of detection limit (S/N = 3). The analytical method provide advantage of sensitivity and specificity for detection of isocarbophos in apple and cabbage.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Ti3C2TX-gold composite
Synthesis
Detection of pesticide
Aptasensor
Food

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Detection of ISO in apple and cabbage

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors acknowledge the financial support from The National Key Research and Development Program of China (No.2021YFA0910203) and National Natural Science Foundation of China (No.21576115). We also would like to acknowledge the work of the Central Laboratory, School of Chemical and Material Engineering, Jiangnan University.

The authors acknowledge the financial support from The National Key Research and Development Program of China (No. 2021YFA0910203 ) and National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 21576115 ). We also would like to acknowledge the work of the Central Laboratory, School of Chemical and Material Engineering, Jiangnan University .

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Synthesis scheme (A), SEM (B), enlarged SEM (C), TEM (D), enlarged TEM (E), HRTEM images (F) and element mappings of Au (G), C (H), Ti (I) and N elements (J) of Ti3C2TX/RS-GQD@Au.

XRD patterns (A) and enlarged patterns (B) of Ti3C2TX and Ti3C2TX/RS-GQD@Au, UV–visible diffuse reflectance spectra (C) of RS-GQD, Ti3C2TX/RS-GQD and Ti3C2TX/RS-GQD@Au, and plots (B) of transformed Kubelka-Munk function vs. energy of light of RS-GQD (D), Ti3C2TX/RS-GQD (E) and Ti3C2TX/RS-GQD@Au in 0.2 M Na2SO4 solution (F).
Construction scheme of ISO electrochemical sensor and sensing principle.

CV (A) and DPV curves (B) of the sensor in the PBS of pH 7.0 in the absence (a) and the presence (b) of 1.0 × 10−11 M ISO, and the plots of peak current caused by 1.0 × 10−11 M ISO vs. the incubation time (C) and H2 concentration (D).

DPV curves (A) in a PBS of pH 7.0 of the proposed sensor incubated for 60 min in the presence of 8 × 10−16, 1 × 10−15, 1 × 10−15, 2 × 10−15, 1 × 10−14, 2 × 10−14, 1 × 10−13, 2 × 10−13, 1 × 10−12, 2 × 10−12 and 1 × 10−11 M ISO (from top to bottom) and plot (B) of Ip vs. LOG[CISO, M].
The results for electrochemical detection of ISO in apple and cabbage (n = 5).

row.entry: The ISO found by proposed method (pM)

head.ce:title: Electrochemical detection of isocarbophos in apple and cabbage with Ti3C2TX/arginine and serine-functionalized graphene quantum dot@gold nanostar aerogel and target cycle dual signal amplification

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Synthetic and Biological Colloids, Ministry of Education, School of Chemical and Material Engineering, School of Life Science and Health Engineering, Jiangnan University, Wuxi 214122, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Synthetic and Biological Colloids, Ministry of Education, School of Chemical and Material Engineering, School of Life Science and Health Engineering, Jiangnan University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Key Laboratory of Synthetic and Biological Colloids, Ministry of Education, School of Chemical and Material Engineering, School of Life Science and Health Engineering, Jiangnan University, Wuxi 214122, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Materials and Chemical Engineering, Ningbo University of Technology, Ningbo 315211, Zhejiang, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Materials and Chemical Engineering, Ningbo University of Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Materials and Chemical Engineering, Ningbo University of Technology, Ningbo 315211, Zhejiang, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The paper reports one strategy for synthesis of Ti3C2TX/arginine and serine-functionalized graphene quantum dot@Au nanostar aerogel (Ti3C2TX/RS-GQD@Au). Positively charged RS-GQDs bound with negatively charged Ti3C2TX sheets to form Ti3C2TX aerogel. Then, HAuCl4 was in situ reduced with ascorbic acid in the presence of L-glutathione to form gold nanostars on Ti3C2TX sheets. The resulting Ti3C2TX/RS-GQD@Au shows one three-dimensional structure. The gold nanostars were homogeneously dispersed on Ti3C2TX sheets. Introduction of RS-GQD realizes construction of Schottky heterojunction. The presence of L-glutathione triggers an asymmetric structural evolution of gold nanocrystals and leads to more exposed high-index crystal facets. Unique structure achieves to high conductivity, structural stability and catalytic activity. Ti3C2TX/RS-GQD@Au was used for construction of electrochemical sensor for detection of isocarbophos coupled with target cycle amplification. The peak current at 0.27 V linearly increases with the increase of isocarbophos concentration between 1 × 10−11 and 8 × 10−16 M with 2.9 × 10−16 M of detection limit (S/N = 3). The analytical method provide advantage of sensitivity and specificity for detection of isocarbophos in apple and cabbage.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Isocarbophos (ISO) was widely used in agricultural production for pest control. The residual ISO in food has caused serious toxicity to human health. Rapid detection of ISO in food become urgent task [1]. The main methods for detection of ISO are GC-MS [2], colorimetric method [3], fluorescent method [4] and electrochemical method [5]. GC-MS offers high sensitivity and selectivity, but it needs expensive equipment and skilled technical workers. Colorimetric method is simple and rapid, but its sensitivity is lower than that of GC-MS. Fluorescent method shows a better sensitivity than colorimetric method, but it suffers from background fluorescence interference from biomacromolecules. Electrochemical methods with nanomaterials received more attention because of easy to operate, low cost and high sensitivity.

The nanomaterials for electrochemical sensors mainly include noble metal such as gold [6], metal oxide [7] and carbon material such as Ti3C2TX [8]. Gold become the most commonly used nanomaterial in electrochemical detection because of high catalytic activity, conductivity and chemical stability, but small gold nanoparticles are easy to aggregate and brings partial loss of high catalytic activity. Metal oxide offers a required catalytic performance for electrochemical detection due to special catalytic capacity and semiconductor property, but poor conductivity limits the improvement of detection sensitivity. Ti3C2TX gives good conductivity and soft two-dimensional structure. The characteristics make Ti3C2TX sheets are easy to self-assemble into conductive Ti3C2TX aerogel with high electron/ion conductivity. Unfortunately, poor catalytic activity restricts its many applications in biosensors [8]. However, combination of Ti3C2TX with gold can better resolve the problem. Great effort was devoted to synthesis of Ti3C2TX-gold nanocomposites [9]. The first approach is to alternately coat Ti3C2TX and gold nanoparticle [10]. Insertion of gold nanoparticles prevents aggregation of Ti3C2TX sheets between different layers, but Ti3C2TX sheets and gold nanoparticles in the same layer always tend to agglomerate. The second approach is to mix Ti3C2TX with gold nanoparticle [11]. Although the use of ultrasonic treatment improves the mixing effects, the approach still is difficult to make highly dispersed Ti3C2TX-gold composite [12]. The third approach is to reduce HAuCl4 with Ti3C2TX sheet. Due to strong repulsion, negatively charged Ti3C2TX sheets and negatively charged AuCl4 - tend to separate from each other. This leads to formation of large gold nanocrystals [13]. The fourth approach is to introduce functional molecule to immobilize Au3+ on Ti3C2TX sheets by coordination. The coordination achieves to fixation of Au3+ on Ti3C2TX sheets and brings an improved dispersion. However, its actual effect on improving dispersion is very limited because only weak van der Waals force exists in between functional molecules and Ti3C2TX sheets. The fifth approach is to combine with semiconductor for construction of Schottky heterojunction. Formation of Schottky heterojunction reduces the energy barrier between Ti3C2TX and semiconductor and results in an improved electron transfer process. This increases the signal to noise ratio and leads to an improved sensitivity [14]. Despite these advances, there are two problems in synthesis of Ti3C2TX-gold composite that need to be solved. One is a large energy barrier between Ti3C2TX and gold hinders high-speed electron transfer between them. Another is gold nanocrystal offers an unideal catalytic activity because the high-index crystal facets were covered by the low-index crystal facets.

The study reports synthesis of Ti3C2TX/arginine and serine-functionalized graphene quantum dot@gold nanostar aerogel (Ti3C2TX/RS-GQD@Au). Introduction of RS-GQD as semiconductor creates Schottky heterojunction to realize high-speed electron transfer between gold and Ti3C2TX. Introduction of L-glutathione increases exposure of high-index crystal facets of gold nanocrystals to improve the catalytic activity. The electrochemical sensor with Ti3C2TX/RS-GQD@Au and target cycle dual signal amplification exhibits ultrahigh sensitivity and selectivity for electrochemical detection of ISO.

H1 (5´-AGCTTGCTGCAGCGATTCTTGATCGCCACAGAGCTCAAGCTTTTTTT-(CH2)6-SH-3´) [15] and H2 (5´-biferrocene-TTGATCGCCACAGAAAAGCTTGAGCTCTGTGGCGATCAAGAATCGCTGCAGCAAGCTTTT CAT-3´) [16] were prepared by SANGON Biotech. Co., Ltd (Shanghai, China).

Typically, 4 g of citric acid was mixed with 0.4 g of arginine and 3.2 g of serine and then heated 180°C for 3 h. The collected RS-GQD crude was dispersed in water to form 100 mg mL−1 dispersion. Followed by dialysis (3500 Da) and freeze-drying to obtain solid RS-GQD sample.

RS-GQD solution (10 mL, 10 mg mL−1) was dropped into Ti3C2TX solution (100 mL, 5 mg mL−1) under stirring, then centrifugated at 4000 rpm for 3 min, washed with water and dried at 80 °C for 1 h. The collected Ti3C2TX/RS-GQD aerogel was again dispersed in 20 mL water under ultrasound. Its 0.2 mL was added into a growth solution composing of 0.18 M hexadecyl trimethyl ammonium chloride (CTAC), 0.06 mM potassium bromide (KBr), 6 μM potassium iodide (KI), 2.72 μM L-glutathione and 0.4 mM HAuCl4. After injected 0.5 mL 0.5 mM ascorbic acid solution under stirring, it was incubated at 30 °C for 3 min, centrifugated at 6000 rpm for 2 min, washed with water and dried at 60 °C in vacuum for 12 h to obtain Ti3C2TX/RS-GQD@Au.

Dropped 5 μL Ti3C2TX/RS-GQD@Au dispersion (2.0 mg mL−1 in 1.0 wt% chitosan) on the pretreated glass carbon electrode surface (GCE, 1 mm) [5] and then dried at room temperature. A 2.5 μM H1 was mixed with the equal volume of 1 mM TCEP solution and incubated at 37 °C for 30 min. Its 5 μL was dropped on the electrode surface, then incubated at 37 °C for 3 h, washed with water and dried in N2 current. A 5 μL of 1.0 mM mercaptohexanol (MCH) solution was dropped on the electrode surface to block residual active sites, then incubation at 37 °C for 4 h, washed with water and dried at 4 °C.

ISO standard solution with different concentration or sample solution was mixed with an equal volume of 5.0 μM H2. Its 5 μL was dropped onto the surface of electrochemical sensor. Then, it was incubated at 37 °C for 1 h, washed with the PBS of pH 7.0 and dried in N2 current in turn. The DPV curve or peak current at 0.27 V of the sensor in the PBS of pH 7.0 was measured on CHI660D electrochemical workstation.

Fig. 1 A presents synthesis scheme of Ti3C2TX/RS-GQD@Au. Firstly, Ti3C2TX/RS-GQD was prepared by dropping RS-GQD solution into Ti3C2TX solution. As Ti3C2TX is negatively charged and RS-GQD is positively charged (see Fig. S1), their mixing leads to self-assembly of Ti3C2TX sheets driven by electrostatic attraction. The formed Ti3C2TX/RS-GQD aerogel was used for synthesis of Ti3C2TX/RS-GQD@Au. Here, CTA+ cations self-assembles into oil-in-water micelles due to high surface activity. Hydrophilic cationic head groups face outside of micelle while lipophilic long-chain alkyl groups faces center of micelles. By electrostatic attraction, positively charged CTA+ micelles were immobilized on surface of negatively charged Ti3C2TX/RS-GQD. This reduces Zeta potential of aerogel. After added HAuCl4, negatively charged AuCl4 - ions were adsorbed on surface of positively charged CTA+ micelle. AuCl4 - anions were reduced into Au0 atoms by ascorbic acid and entered into interior of CTA+ micelles. This leads to in situ formation of gold nanocrystals on Ti3C2TX sheets. Meanwhile, L-glutathione triggers an asymmetric structural evolution of gold nanocrystals to improve exposure of high-index crystal facets [17].

Fig. 1 B-J present SEM, TEM images and element mappings of Ti3C2TX/RS-GQD@Au. Ti3C2TX/RS-GQD@Au shows one three-dimensional structure with the rich of pores. The gold nanocrystals were well dispersed on Ti3C2TX sheets. The gold nanocrystals display star-like nanostructure with sharp wheels and corners. HRTEM image contains two spacings of 0.238 nm, corresponding to the (002) plane of Ti3C2TX and 0.182 nm, corresponding to the (100) plane of gold nanocrystals. The element mappings reveal spatial distribution of Au, C, Ti and N elements in Ti3C2TX/RS-GQD@Au.

Fig. S2 presents total, Ti3p, Ti3s, Au4f, C1s, N1s, Ti2p, O1s and Ti2s XPS spectra of Ti3C2TX/RS-GQD@Au. Total XPS spectrum shows Ti3C2TX/RS-GQD@Au consists of Ti, Au, C, N and O elements. Au4f XPS spectrum includes four peaks at 83.6, 84.6, 87.3 and 87.9 eV. The peaks at 83.6 and 87.3 eV correspond to Au4f5/2 and Au4f7/2 of Au0. The peaks at 84.6 and 87.9 eV correspond to Au 4 f5/2 and Au 4 f7/2 of Au+, verifying the existence of Au0 and Au+ species. C1s XPS spectrum includes four peaks at 281.6, 284.9, 285.9 and 287.6 eV, corresponding to Ti-C, C-C, C-N and O-C bonds [18]. N1s XPS spectrum includes three peaks at 396.7, 397.8 and 398.7 eV, corresponding to Ti-N, N-C and N-Ti-O bonds. Ti2p XPS spectrum includes six peaks at 455.1, 456.2, 459.3, 461.2, 462.3 and 464.1 eV. The peaks at 455.1 and 461.2 eV correspond to Ti2p3/2 and Ti2p1/2 of Ti-C bond. The peaks at 456.2 and 462.3 eV correspond to Ti2p3/2 and Ti2p1/2 of Ti3+/Ti2+. The peaks at 459.3 and 459.3 eV correspond to Ti2p3/2 and Ti2p1/2 of Ti-O bond. O 1 s XPS spectrum includes four peaks at 526.7, 527.2, 527.8 and 529.1 eV, corresponding to Ti-O, Ti-C-O, Ti-OH and C-OH bonds [19].

Fig. 2 A and B present XRD patterns of Ti3C2TX and Ti3C2TX/RS-GQD@Au. XRD pattern of Ti3C2TX/RS-GQD@Au includes nine peaks at 5.98 °, 38.18°, 44.36°, 64.61°, 77.47°, 81.72°, 98.1°, 110.82° and 115.32°. The peak at 5.98° correspond to the (002) crystal face of Ti3C2Tx. Compared with Ti3C2TX, the peak displays a lower diffraction intensity and a smaller 2θ value. This is due to formation of Ti3C2TX aerogel [20]. Poor crystallinity and low mass ratio of RS-GQD make the peak at about 26° of graphene is too weak to be seen. The peaks at 38.18°, 44.36°, 64.61°, 77.47°, 81.72°, 98.1°, 110.82° and 115.32° correspond to the (111), (200), (220), (311), (222), (400), (311) and (420) facets of fcc gold nanocrystal (No. JCPDS 4–0784). For comparison, another Ti3C2TX/RS-GQD@Au sample was prepared by the same procedure unless no addition of L-glutathione (Ti3C2TX/RS-GQD@Au-0). Table S1 shows Ti3C2TX/RS-GQD@Au offers more exposed high-index facets compared with Ti3C2TX/RS-GQD@Au-0. This improves the catalytic activity [21].

Fig. S3 presents Raman spectrum of Ti3C2TX/RS-GQD@Au. The Raman peaks between 100 and 750 cm−1 are the characteristic Raman peaks of Ti3C2TX. Two broad Raman peaks at 1312 cm−1 and 1579 correspond to the characteristic D peak and G peak of graphene. The above results demonstrate that Ti3C2TX/RS-GQD@Au contains Ti3C2TX and graphene (RS-GQD).

Fig. 2 C-F present UV–visible diffuse reflectance spectra and plots of transformed Kubelka-Munk function vs. energy of light of RS-GQD, Ti3C2TX/RS-GQD and Ti3C2TX/RS-GQD@Au. RS-GQD exhibits strong absorption capacity to ultraviolet and visible light. However, the light absorption band widens but the intensity decreases after introducted Ti3C2TX and gold nanocrystal. This is because high conductivity of Ti3C2TX and gold nanocrystal accelerate the energy transfer from RS-GQD to Ti3C2TX and gold nanocrystal. The plots of transformed Kubelka-Munk function vs. the energy of light of RS-GQD indicate one positive slope, verifying that RS-GQD, Ti3C2TX/RS-GQD and Ti3C2TX/RS-GQD@Au are n-type semiconductors [22]. The band gap values (Eg) were estimated basing on the plots in Fig. 2D-F. Ti3C2TX/RS-GQD@Au has the smallest Eg value (0.50 eV), which is smaller than that of RS-GQD (2.208 eV) and Ti3C2TX/RS-GQD (0.60 eV). This demonstrates the introduction of Ti3C2TX and gold nanocrystal can reduce the Eg value. This is because the intimate contact of RS-GQD with Ti3C2TX and gold nanocrystal leads to construction of Schottky heterojunction at Ti3C2TX/RS-GQD/gold interface. The presence of Schottky heterojunction decreases the Eg value and leads to a better conductivity. To confirm the suggest, the Nyquist plots of RS-GQD, Ti3C2TX/RS-GQD and Ti3C2TX/RS-GQD@Au were measured (see Fig. S4). Their charge transfer resistance (Rct) values were calculated by basing on the equivalent circuit. The Rct values are 348.1 Ω of RS-GQD, 0.47 Ω of Ti3C2TX/RS-GQD and 0.152 Ω of Ti3C2TX/RS-GQD@Au. The result indicates that the formation of Schottky heterojunction achieves to 2290-fold increase in the conductivity.

The sensor construction includes three steps (shown in Fig. 3). Firstly, Ti3C2TX/RS-GQD@Au was immobilized on GCE surface using chitosan as adhesive. Then, -SH group modified H1 was covalently connected to Ti3C2TX/RS-GQD@Au/GCE surface via Au-S bond. Finally, MCH was used for blocking residual active sites. To understand the construction scheme, CV and EIS behaviors of different electrodes were investigated in 1.0 mM K4Fe(CN)6 in the PBS of pH 7.0. Fig. S5 indicates that the introduction of Ti3C2TX/RS-GQD@Au produces an obviously increased CV current. This is due to high catalytic activity of Ti3C2TX/RS-GQD@Au. However, the immobilization of H1 and MCH brings an obviously decreased CV current with an increased Rct value. This is because H1 and MCH are electrochemical inert. Their modification partly blocks the channels for electron transfer between electrolyte and electrode and leads to a reduce in the CV current and an increase in the Rct value. The results of CV and EIS analysis demonstrate that the proposed sensor has been successfully constructed.

H1 and H2 were designed to achieve sensitive and selective electrochemical detection of ISO. H1 includes one hairpin structure and one aptamer of ISO. The hairpin structure was formed by hybridization of the first six bases at 5′ end with the last six bases at 3′ end via base pairing. The aptamer is one base sequence composed of the sixth base to 41st base at 5′ end. H2 includes one hairpin structure, one base sequence that can hybridize with H1 and one redox probe (ferrocene). The hairpin structure was formed by hybridization of the first fourteen bases at 5′ end with the fourteen bases of the 25th base to the 39th base at 5′ end via base pairing. The redox probe (ferrocene molecule) was covalently connected to H2 by C-O bond [16].

In the absence of ISO, the base sequence in H1 that can pair with H2 was embedded in inside of the hairpin structure. Thus, the hairpin H1 cannot combine with H2 to bring ferrocene molecules to the electrode surface. However, the hairpin structure of H1 can be opened by ISO. In the presence of ISO, ISO molecule binds with the aptamer in H1 to form stable composite [15]. With the formation of the composite, the hairpin structure of H1 will be firmly opened. The opened H1 hybridizes with H2 to form double stranded DNA basing on the principle of base pairing. Since the hybridization energy between H1 and H2 is higher than the binding energy between H1 and ISO, the ISO in composite can be reliably replaced by H2. Consequently, the above process can achieve to the immobilization of H2 on the electrode surface and. This leads to one ferrocene molecule in H2 being brought to the electrode surface. The redox of ferrocene molecule on the electrode surface will produce sensitive electrochemical response. The released ISO will open another H1, triggering the next target cycle process. By the target (ISO) cycle, one ISO molecule can bring N ferrocene molecules to the electrode surface and leads to a significantly signal amplification.

Three control experiments were designed to examine the above sensing principle. The first control experiment is that the proposed sensor was incubated in a mixed solution of H1 and H2 in the absence and the presence of 1.0 × 10−11 M ISO at 37 °C for 60 min Fig. 4A and B show in the absence of ISO no obvious redox peaks appear in the CV and DPV curves. This is because the target cycle cannot be triggered in the absence of ISO. As a result, no ferrocene molecules can be brought to the electrode surface, indicating a poor CV and DPV response. However, the presence of ISO brings an obvious increase of CV and DPV current. This is because ISO can trigger the target cycle. Consequently, one ISO molecule can bring N ferrocene molecules to the electrode surface. Their redox produce a significantly signal amplification, indicating a sensitive CV and DPV current response. Fig. 4A and B also indicates that the DPV detection has a better current response than the CV measurement. Thus, the DPV technique was selected for electrochemical detection of ISO. The second control experiment is that the proposed sensor was incubated in a 2.5 μM of the unmodified ferrocene H2 in the absence and the presence of 1.0 × 10−11 M ISO at 37 °C for 60 min Fig. S6A shows the presence of ISO cannot bring an obvious change on the DPV current. This demonstrates that the DPV signal of proposed sensor is attributed to the redox of ferrocene molecules. The third control experiment is that the proposed sensor was incubated in 2.5 μM ferrocene solution at 37 °C for 60 min Fig. S6B shows no peak appears in the DPV curve, indicating no ferrocene molecules were immobilized on the electrode surface during the incubation. The result demonstrates that the non-specific adsorption of ferrocene molecules on the electrode surface can be ignored. The above results demonstrate that the proposed sensor can be used for electrochemical detection of ISO.

To optimize the conditions for electrochemical detection of ISO, influence of incubation time and H2 concentration on DPV peak current (Ip) was investigated. Fig. 4C presents the plot of Ip vs. the incubation time. The increase of incubation time leads to a rapid increase of Ip when the time is less than 30 min. This is because more incubation time can complete more target cycles. This leads to more ferrocene molecules being brought to the electrode surface. Their redox produces a higher Ip. However, the increase slows down and then reaches the maximum when the time is less than 60 min, indicating the target cycle reaches to equilibrium. In the state, number of ferrocene molecules transferred to the electrode surface is the same as number of ferrocene molecules left from the electrode surface. Thus, the Ip no longer increases with the incubation time. To save time and obtain high sensitivity, the incubation time of 1 h was selected for detection of ISO.

Fig. 4D presents the plot of Ip vs. the H2 concentration. The Ip increases with the increase of H2 concentration when the H2 concentration is less than 2.5 μM. As number of H2 in the system is less than that required by the target cycle. With increasing H2 concentration, more ferrocene molecules are brought to the electrode surface. This leads to an increased Ip. But the Ip value reaches the maximum when the H2 concentration is more than 2.5 μM. This confirms the use of 2.5 μM H2 can meet the need of target cycle for H2. Continue to increase H2 concentration cannot increase number of ferrocene molecules brought by the target cycle. Thus, the Ip does not change with H2 concentration. Herein, the H2 concentration of 5 μM was selected for detection of ISO.

Fig. 5 A presents the DPV curves in the presence of different concentration of ISO. The DPV current increases with increasing ISO. A higher ISO concentration produces a faster target cycle. This leads to more ferrocene molecules being brought to the electrode surface. Their redox produces a higher DPV peak current. Further, the plot of DPV peak current (Ip, nA) vs. the logarithm value of ISO concentration (LOG[CISO, M]) was shown in Fig. 5B. There is a good linear relationship between Ip and LOG[CISO, M] when the ISO concentration is between 8 × 10−16 and 1 × 10−11 M. The linear regression equation is: Ip (nA)= 235.96 ×LOG[CISO, M]+ 3549.4, with the correlation coefficient (R2) of 0.997. The limit of detection (LOD) was obtained basing on 3 times of signal-to-noise ratio. The LOD value is 2.9 × 10−16 M. The result shows the proposed sensor has a much higher sensitivity compared with other methods in Table S2.

Twenty sensors were constructed and their DPV peak current (Ip) responses towards 2 × 10−12 M ISO were measured by the same procedure. The relative standard deviation (RSD) of Ip values for twenty sensors is 1.9%, indicating good reproducibility. The same sensor was used for twenty consecutive detection of 2 × 10−12 M ISO. The RSD of Ip values for the consecutive detections is 2.3%, indicating high repeatability. The sensor was stored at 4 ℃ and taken out every two days for DPV detection of 2 × 10−12 M ISO. The RSD of Ip values for 30 days storage period is 1.7%, indicating good storage stability.

The influence of inorganic ions, organic small molecules and pesticides that may exist in fruit and vegetable on detection of 2 × 10−12 M ISO was investigated. The inorganic ions of 1 mM causes less than 1% Ip change, including Na+, K+, Ca2+, Mg2+, Fe3+, Cu2+, Zn2+, HCO3 -, C2O4 2-, PO4 3- and SO4 2-. These inorganic ions cannot be oxidized and reduced at 0.27 V, thus their presence cannot bring an obvious Ip change. Some organic small molecules are electrochemically active species, but 10 mM of citric acid, malic acid, glucose and ascorbic acid causes less than 2% Ip change. This is because these organic small molecules cannot trigger the target cycle. The co-existing pesticides are the most likely to interfere with detection of ISO, because of their similar structure and property. Fig. S7 shows the Ip values in the presence of chlorpyrifos (CHL), omethoate (OME), carbendazim (CAR), phoxim (PHO), imidacloprid (IMI), avermectin (AVE), acetamiprid (ACE) and ISO. Only ISO can produce a high Ip value. This verifies that the proposed sensor has high selectivity for electrochemical detection of ISO.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of ISO in apple and cabbage
ce:section.ce:para: The apple and cabbage samples were collected from Supermarket at Wuxi city and pretreated by the reported method [xx]. The obtained sample solutions were used for electrochemical detection of ISO by the proposed method and GC-MS. Table 1 shows that the results obtained by the proposed method are consistent with that of GC-MS, and recoveries are in the range of 96.4–103.6%. These demonstrate that the proposed method has good precision and accuracy.

We demonstrated synthesis of Ti3C2TX/RS-GQD@Au. The introduction of RS-GQD and L-glutathione achieves to construction of three-dimensional structure and Schottky heterojunction and gold nanocrystals with more exposed high-index facets. The synthesis leads to an improved catalytic activity. The electrochemical sensor with Ti3C2TX/RS-GQD@Au and target cycle amplification exhibits ultrahigh sensitivity, selectivity and repeatability for electrochemical detection of ISO.
Li Ruiyi: Investigation and writing. Wang Nan: Investigation. Shen Yirui: Investigation. Wang Guangli: Investigation. Li Zaijun: Conceptualization, Methodology.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors acknowledge the financial support from The National Key Research and Development Program of China (No.2021YFA0910203) and National Natural Science Foundation of China (No.21576115). We also would like to acknowledge the work of the Central Laboratory, School of Chemical and Material Engineering, Jiangnan University.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.snb.2023.134162.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
Supplementary material
.

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Li Ruiyi is currently one associate professor in School of Pharmaceutical Sciences, Jiangnan University, China. She received his master degree from The Birmingham University, The United Kingdom, 2014. She received his doctor degree from Jiangnan University, China, 2018. Her research interest focus on the synthesis of nanomaterials and their application in analytical chemistry.

Wang Nan is currently Master candidate in School of Chemical and Material Engineering, Jiangnan University, China. Her research interest is the synthesis of nanomaterials and their application in electrochemical sensors.

Shen Yirui is School of Materials and Chemical Engineering, Ningbo University of Technology, Ningbo 315211, Zhejiang, China. Her research interest is synthesis of graphene-based composite and their application in electrochemical sensors.

Wang Guangli is one professor of School of Chemical and Materials Engineering, Jiangnan University, China. He received his doctor degree from Nanjing University, China. His current research interests are synthesis of metal nanomaterials and their application in electrochemical sensors.

Li Zaijun is one professor of School of Chemical and Materials Engineering, Jiangnan University, China. He received his doctor degree from Jiangnan University, China, 2004. He is author of more than 300 papers and reviewer of more than 40 journals. His current research interests are synthesis of graphene-based nanomaterials and their applications in sensing, electrocatalysis and energy storage/conversion devices.
